prompts.text: List the five most commonly used sorting algorithms. For each, state the average time complexity and one practical use case.

I would like you to please note that I need you to basically summarize the key differences between REST and GraphQL APIs. It is important to note that due to the fact that a large number of developers are using both approaches, in order to make a well-informed decision, as you know, the comparison needs to cover performance, flexibility, and ease of use. Essentially, I want you to provide a clear, detailed breakdown in order to help our team understand which approach is better for our use case.

You are a senior distributed systems engineer. Below is a complete technical specification for a high-throughput event processing platform.

SECTION 1: SYSTEM OVERVIEW
The platform ingests events from upstream producers at a sustained rate of 500,000 events per second with burst tolerance up to 2,000,000 events per second for intervals not exceeding 30 seconds. Each event is a JSON document between 256 bytes and 16 kilobytes. Events must be durably stored within 50 milliseconds of receipt, enriched with metadata within 200 milliseconds, and made available to downstream consumers within 500 milliseconds end-to-end. The system must sustain 99.99% availability measured over rolling 30-day windows.

SECTION 2: INGESTION LAYER
The ingestion layer consists of stateless HTTP/2 and gRPC endpoints deployed across three availability zones. Each endpoint validates event schema against a registry, assigns a monotonically increasing sequence number within its shard, computes a SHA-256 content hash for deduplication, and writes to a WAL on local NVMe storage before acknowledging the producer. The WAL is replicated synchronously to one standby node and asynchronously to a third replica. Producers use exponential backoff with jitter when they receive 429 or 503 responses. The load balancer uses consistent hashing on producer ID to maintain session affinity, reducing duplicate detection overhead.

SECTION 3: STORAGE SUBSYSTEM
Primary storage is a custom LSM-tree engine optimized for append-heavy workloads. Memtables are 64 MB each; L0 compaction triggers at 4 SSTables; L1 target size is 256 MB with a 10x multiplier per subsequent level. Bloom filters with 1% false-positive rate protect against unnecessary disk reads. All data is compressed with Zstandard level 3 before being written to disk. Checksums using CRC32C are verified on every read. A secondary columnar store receives a copy of all events via a replication stream for analytical queries; it is updated within 5 seconds and stores data in Parquet format partitioned by event type and UTC hour.

SECTION 4: ENRICHMENT PIPELINE
After durable storage, events enter an enrichment pipeline consisting of five stages executed as a directed acyclic graph. Stage 1 resolves producer identity against an internal registry backed by a read-through cache with a 60-second TTL. Stage 2 applies geo-IP lookup using a locally cached MaxMind database refreshed every 24 hours. Stage 3 evaluates a rule engine with up to 200 active rules per event type; rules are compiled to bytecode and cached per event schema version. Stage 4 computes derived fields including session identifiers, funnel position, and cohort assignments using deterministic hash functions. Stage 5 emits enriched events to a Kafka topic with exactly-once semantics enabled via transactional producers.

SECTION 5: DELIVERY AND CONSUMER API
Consumers subscribe to filtered streams using a SQL-like predicate language supporting equality, range, IN, LIKE, and IS NULL operators on any enriched field. The platform maintains per-consumer offset checkpoints in a distributed key-value store with 3-way replication. Consumers may replay events from any offset within the 30-day retention window. Delivery uses server-sent events for low-latency push and a pull API for batch consumers. The pull API supports cursor-based pagination, returns up to 10,000 events per request, and supports field projection to reduce payload size. Webhooks are supported for external integrations; failed deliveries are retried with exponential backoff up to 72 hours before being sent to a dead-letter queue.

Analyze this specification and produce: (1) an identification of the three highest-risk architectural components, (2) a capacity planning model for the ingestion layer assuming 10 geographic regions each handling equal load, and (3) a failure mode and effects analysis for the enrichment pipeline covering single-node failure, network partition, and upstream registry unavailability.

## Project Requirements

**Background**: I would like you to please note that due to the fact that our system processes a large number of requests, essentially we need you to design a comprehensive architecture. It is important to note that in order to meet our SLA requirements, basically the solution must be scalable.

## Detailed Specifications

**Performance**: The system must handle *at least* 10,000 requests per second. Please ensure that in the event that load spikes occur, the architecture can scale horizontally. As you may know, a large number of cloud providers offer auto-scaling, which from my perspective is the preferred approach.

**Reliability**: Due to the fact that downtime costs approximately $50,000 per hour, in order to maintain availability, the system must achieve 99.99% uptime. With regard to failure modes, please note that each component must have redundancy.

**Security**: I need you to basically implement end-to-end encryption. In my opinion, with respect to data at rest, AES-256 is the standard. Please be aware that all inter-service communication must use mutual TLS.

**Observability**: I would like you to provide detailed logging, in order to enable tracing across services. A majority of incidents due to the fact that they go undetected are a result of insufficient monitoring. Essentially, implement distributed tracing with correlation IDs.

Provide a complete architecture diagram description, technology stack recommendations, and implementation roadmap.
